project.title: TMT-Based quantitative proteomics reveals the genetic mechanisms of secondary hair follicle development in the fine wool sheep foetus
project.projectDescription: Chinese Merino Sheep (Junken Type) is one of the fine wool sheep breeds in China, which is famous for producing fine wool. Wool quality including fibre diameter and length is the most concerned economic indicator for producers. Growth and development of secondary hair follicles (SFs) influences the wool quality. Understanding the formation mechanism of wool quality and related key proteins can help optimize and control the production of wool. However, The mechanism by which proteins mediate the development of SFs in the sheep foetus remains elusive. Here, based on the histomorphology of the SFs among four stages of foetus development (E75, E85, E95 and E105), the differential protein profiles of skin tissues were determined in three comparison pairs of foetus development (E75 versus E85, E85 versus E95, and E95 versus E105) by TMT-Based quantitative proteomics technology. With the help of proteomics technology, a total of 238, 35 and 348 differentially expressed proteins (DEPs) were identified in three comparison periods, respectively. In SFs development, focal adhesion, ECM−receptor interaction and estrogen signaling pathway, etc. played important role. COL1A1, THBS3, ITGA6, COL6A1 and THBS4 were potential candidate proteins in SF initiation. Overall, this study provided valuable proteomics data on the SFs development and a prospective understanding of the molecular mechanisms for wool quality traits in fine wool sheep.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Skin tissues were frozen and pulverized in liquid nitrogen, and total proteins was extracted by with 8M urea, 0.1% SDS, protease inhibitors PMSF, followed by ultrasonic method. Protein concentration was measured by BCA Protein Assay Kit (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA), and SDS-PAGE gel electrophoresis was performed with 12% (w/v) acrylamide discontinuous gels. Electrophoresis of proteins was performed at 90V for 40min in 12% sample gel, then 120V for 120min in separating gel. The dye for visualizing proteins was staining with Coomassie Brilliant Blue R250.100μg protein samples were taken from each sample and digested with trypsin by FASP. The liquid was collected after enzymatic hydrolysis and dried. 100μg peptide was obtained from each sample for labeling. The sample processing and labeling were completed according to the protocol of Pierce TMT ® Mass Tagging Kits and Reagents Kit, then mixed labeled samples were used for C18 column classification. The operation of classification was performed according to the HPLC procedures. Then, sample was injected into the liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) system for analysis by using Thermo Scientific Q Exactive mass spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The ratio of reported ions was quantified to generate the original mass spectrometry data-file.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: The MS/MS data were conducted using Proteome Discoverer 1.4 (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) against the UniProt sequence database (Ovis aries) for peptide identification and quantification. The highly reliable qualitative results of peptide and protein mass spectrometry were obtained at 1% FDR. The DAVID software would be used to annotated GO functional based on protein sequence alignment method. Then proteins were classified by Gene Ontology annotation based on biological process, cellular component and molecular function. Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database was used to annotate protein pathway. Proteins were classified by GO annotation into three categories for each category, a t-tests was employed to test the enrichment of the differentially expressed protein against all identified proteins. The GO with a corrected p-value < 0.05 is considered significant. Enrichment of pathway analysis: Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database was used to identify enriched pathways by a t-tests to test the enrichment of the differentially expressed protein against all identified proteins. The pathway with a corrected p-value < 0.05 was considered significant. A fold change > 1.5 or fold change < 0.67 and statistical significance (P-value < 0.05) were used to identify differentially expressed proteins (DEPs). All DEPs database accession or sequence were searched against the STRING database version 11.0 for protein-protein interactions.

references.referenceLine: Qiu LX, Yu Q, Zhou HQ, Fan WH, Zheng JJ, Yang YL, Zhang WZ, Cao X, Yang H. TMT-based quantitative proteomics reveals the genetic mechanisms of secondary hair follicle development in fine-wool sheep. PLoS One. 2025 20(2):e0315637

Characteristics[Specimen]: Skin samples were collected from the two sides of each fetus and then divided into two parts; one part was frozen in liquid nitrogen for proteomics analysis, and the other was fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for HF histomorphology.
Characteristics[NumberOfBiologicalReplic: Three fetuses were collected at each of four sampling dates (75d (E75), 85d (E85), 95d (E95), and 105d (E105)) of gestation according to known periods of HF development.
Characteristics[NumberOfTechnicalReplica: analyzed in triplicate for LC-MS/MS identification.
Comment[Instrument]: Thermo Scientific Q Exactive mass spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA).
Comment[AcquisitionMethod]: The parent ion of ionic strength TOP25 was selected for secondary mass spectrometry identification.
Comment[FractionationMethod]: mixed labeled samples were used for C18 column classification.
Comment[NumberOfFractions]: to form a total of 20 components.
